text: The surgeon places four 8mm trocars and incises the peritoneum on the right side of the rectum, positioned medial to the right ureter and lateral to the right hypogastric nerve, creating a peritoneal flap for suspension.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectum in a circumferential manner, beginning posteriorly through the holy plane and advancing towards the pouch of Douglas.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectum circumferentially, beginning posteriorly through the holy plane and advancing towards the pouch of Douglas.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum between the vagina and the rectum, deepening the dissection in this area.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum between the vagina and the rectum, deepening the dissection to further mobilize these structures.
The surgeon identifies the levator ani muscles to define the extent of pelvic mobilization during the rectopexy procedure.

The surgeon verifies hemostasis and proceeds to dissect the sacral promontory, carefully freeing it from surrounding structures including the lumbosacral trunk laterally and the hypogastric nerves medially.

The surgeon fixates the peritoneal flaps of the rectum to the sacral promontory using non-absorbable braided interrupted tuo sutures on both sides.
The surgeon fixates the peritoneal flaps of the rectum to the sacral promontory using non-absorbable braided interrupted sutures bilaterally, securing the rectum in place.
The surgeon performs a dissection to the left of the rectum, continuing the circumferential mobilization within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon performs a dissection to the left of the rectum, continuing the circumferential mobilization initiated in the previous steps.
The surgeon closes the opening in the peritoneum using non-absorbable running sutures, as indicated by an arrow in the surgical field.
